chapters.title: Chapter 1: Three Names
chapters.text:
The network status board showed three red indicators at 0701.

Iris was at the coordination terminal when the first went red, and the second, and the third — each less than a minute apart, which was not how failure was supposed to work. Hardware failed when something broke. Administrative holds were filed during business hours. Evacuations were emergencies that announced themselves. None of the three failures shared a cause or a logic or a geography. They arrived simultaneously because the network was new and its weak points were new and no one had yet learned which pressures would arrive together.

She read the first incident report. She read the second. She read the third. By the time she had finished the third, she understood that each failure was a different kind of problem and that there was no single response that addressed all three.

That was the morning.

---

The first incident report was from the eastern relay — the relay node that connected the eastern communities' parallel records to the distributed network. The cause was hardware: equipment failure in the relay's primary switching unit. The equipment was eight years old. It had been flagged for replacement twice in the Archive's maintenance queue. The first flag had been deprioritized because the eastern communities were not at that time part of the Archive's active service area. The second flag had been entered fourteen months ago, when the reconciliation framework began, and had sat in the queue since.

The node operator's name was Solan. His incident report was brief and precise:

*Relay offline 0658. Cause: switching unit failure, expected based on component age. Replacement parts requested three months ago via eastern communities infrastructure request queue. Request status: pending. Alternate routing: none available without Archive eastern corridor access. Community parallel records: isolated. Node operator status: available but unable to restore without parts. Requesting expedited parts delivery or alternate routing.*

Iris read this and made two notes: one to the parts queue for immediate escalation, one to the eastern corridor routing team. Then she opened Solan's infrastructure request from three months ago and confirmed it was where he said it was: in the queue, pending, exactly as submitted, not reviewed.

The parts request had been pending for three months because the eastern corridor was not a priority infrastructure zone. The eastern corridor was not a priority infrastructure zone because the communities there had been outside the Archive's formal service area for eight years. They had been outside the formal service area because — she stopped that chain of reasoning before she reached its end, because she had followed it before and the end was the same every time, and following it again would not change the relay being offline.

She sent Solan a direct message at 0719: *Parts request escalated to priority routing. Alternate eastern corridor path under review — outcome uncertain. Node offline confirmed. Your documentation is complete and accurate. Working on resolution.*

She did not tell him how long it would take, because she did not know. The parts were in a supply depot two districts east of the eastern communities. The eastern corridor access route was not formally available to the non-Archive network; it was the infrastructure Essek's twelve nodes had come online through, and whether it could be extended to cover a parts delivery was a question she had not asked yet. She had added it to the list of questions.

She escalated the parts request and moved to the second incident report.

---

The second incident report came from the mountain settlement's node. The mountain settlement's node had been maintained since the network's founding by a woman named Ilena — Ilen in the coordination records, where she had been listed since vol-028's first briefing. The incident report had been submitted automatically when the node went offline and had been filled in by the network's automated monitoring, which had included a note from the coordination contact that the mountain settlement had forwarded.

The note said: *Node operator evacuated from home timeline following secondary collapse event. Status: safe. Location: resettlement facility, exact facility not yet confirmed. Node: offline. Word for seasonal ceremony: no active registry. Word for seasonal ceremony: in ceased category, three variants, semantic content recorded as unrecoverable.*

Iris read the phrase *safe* and held it for a moment. Then she read the rest. The word for the seasonal ceremony — the one she had added to the *ceased* category in the first days of the network, when the losses were arriving before she had language for them — had been in no living memory except the mountain settlement's operator. The operator had been evacuated. The three phonetic variants in the registry were what remained. That had not changed. The ceased entry was still correct: semantic content unrecoverable. The word was in three variants and no memory.

What had changed was that the network link was down. The ceased entry existed in 34 nodes. It was not going to be further lost. But the node's other contents — the mountain settlement's active registry, the living records, the ongoing testimony — were now offline along with it, unreachable until the node operator returned or someone else took over the node role.

She added Ilen's name to a separate list. Not an incident report. A different kind of record: people the network needed to contact personally when they could be found. Ilen had been evacuated to a resettlement facility — that much was in the coordination contact's note, but the specific facility was not yet confirmed. Resettlement facilities were not always easy to contact through standard network channels, because they were often in regions with degraded relay coverage, which was part of why people needed to be resettled there. The irony of this was not abstract.

The network could not restore what had been in Ilen's memory. It could not reach her until someone identified which facility had received her. It could not bring the mountain settlement's node back online without either her return or another operator trained and authorized for the node. These were three separate problems, each one requiring a different person's specific action. She wrote all three of them down under Ilen's name on the contact list.

She moved to the third incident report.

---

The third incident report was from the Archive district's liaison office. It was not technically an incident — it was a formal administrative notification: the Archive district node had been placed under classification review by order of the district's archivist council, pending an internal review of the node's registry contents. The review was expected to take six to eight weeks. During that period, the node's external network connections would be suspended.

The legal language was straightforward. The hold was valid under Archive administrative code. The district's archivist council had the authority to conduct classification reviews of network nodes that held Archive-originated records, and the hold was correctly documented.

Iris read the filing name. *Council Archivist Tomas Vael, District Registry Authority.*

She checked the node's registry contents against the hold notice and found what she had expected: the node segment under review contained calibration records from a period in Act III when the Archive's district operations had included calibration events in the Juncture corridor. The calibration records were complete. The events they documented were real. The people who had authorized them were named in the records.

One of those names was Tomas Vael.

She marked the hold as *active — legal — contested* in the incident log. Then she flagged it for Corin's attention with a note: *Administrative hold filed by individual whose name appears in records the hold affects. Potential conflict of interest. Requires response outside standard incident protocol.*

She had filed three incident reports in forty-two minutes. The three failures were unrelated to each other in cause. They converged on the same morning because the network was young enough that its failure modes were still finding each other.

---

Corin arrived at the coordination terminal at 0748. He read the three incident reports without asking her to summarize them. When he finished, he looked at the map on the wall — the one with nodes marked in colors: green for active, red for offline, yellow for degraded connectivity. The map had three new red nodes.

"Different problems," he said.

"Yes."

"Are you working on all three simultaneously?"

"I have escalations in place for Solan's parts and a personal contact note for Ilen. The third one needs you."

He looked at the flag on Vael's hold. "Tomas Vael is in the calibration records?"

"As an authorizing signatory."

"A review he filed that covers the records naming him."

"Yes."

He sat down at the adjacent terminal and began reading the original calibration records — the full documents, not just the index. She let him read. This was not a thing she could summarize usefully. The records said what they said. The authorization chains were what they were.

At 0831, he finished and looked up. "I'm going to route this to the Archive's internal review office. Not the district office. If Vael filed the hold, the district office is his. We need a different track."

"How long will that take?"

"Days, minimum. Weeks if they want to be thorough."

"The node data is inaccessible while the hold runs."

"I know." He looked at the map. "Is anything in that node segment uniquely vulnerable?"

She checked the registry contents again. "There are three testimony entries from the Juncture district — people who gave testimony about the calibration events and whose accounts are not duplicated in other nodes yet. We pushed to 34 nodes at the time of distribution. The hold was filed after distribution, which means those entries are only in nodes that received them before the hold took effect."

"How many nodes hold those specific entries?"

She ran the query. "Eleven."

"Eleven out of 34," Corin said. He was quiet for a moment. "Not the same as one, but not safe either."

"No."

He sent the internal review request to the Archive's oversight office and flagged it with the conflict of interest notation she had prepared. Then he sent a separate message to three contacts in the Juncture district community — people who had given testimony, whose entries were in those eleven nodes, who deserved to know that their records were under a hold filed by someone named in the calibration events their testimony described.

The message was brief. He told them what had happened and what he had done and what he did not yet know. He did not minimize the hold or promise that it would be lifted quickly. He told them what was true, which was that their entries were in eleven nodes and that the network was continuing to work.

---

Solan sent a reply to her 0719 message at 0849: *Thank you. Will maintain station until parts arrive. Node equipment catalogued for replacement. Parallel records will be intact when relay restores.*

She saved the message to the incident record. The parallel records would be intact. Solan had been maintaining them on borrowed parts for two years. He knew how to keep something alive longer than the infrastructure said it could stay alive. He would keep them until the network could reach him.

By 0900, the incident log had three entries. Each one was complete: the cause, the response, the name attached.

*Incident 029-001: Eastern relay hardware failure. Node operator: Solan. Parts escalation in process. Alternate routing under review.*

*Incident 029-002: Mountain settlement node offline — operator evacuated. Node operator: Ilen (Ilena — resettlement facility location pending). Word for seasonal ceremony: ceased category, three variants, no memory. Node offline. Personal contact required.*

*Incident 029-003: Archive district node — administrative hold filed by Council Archivist Tomas Vael, whose name appears in the records under review. Hold contested. Internal oversight referral filed.*

Three names. Three different failures. The network had a record now of each one — not resolutions, because none of them were resolved yet, but accounts. The network's first entry on how it failed was honest about the shape of the failure: technical, human, institutional, each one distinct, each one requiring something the others did not.

Corin looked at the incident log at 0900 and said nothing for a moment.

Then he said: "We need a standard format for incidents. One that can hold all three of these in the same structure without pretending they're the same problem."

"I'm working on it," Iris said.

She was. The incident format she had used for the three entries that morning was ad hoc — the archive's standard incident template, adapted in the moment to include fields that the template had not been designed for. The template had a *cause* field and a *status* field and a *recommended action* field. It did not have a *name attached* field, because the archive's standard incident management system was designed for infrastructure events, not for the human texture of what it meant when a relay failed because parts sat in a queue for three months, or when an operator was evacuated and the word she held in memory went with her.

She would need to build a field for names. Not because the technical information was insufficient — it was accurate — but because the accurate information left out the thing that made each failure distinct. Solan was maintaining his station until the parts arrived. Ilen was in a resettlement facility that no one had yet confirmed. Tomas Vael had filed a hold on records that named him. Those were people, not data points, and the incident log would need to hold them the way she had built the witness registry to hold names: as the specific human content of a situation that would otherwise read as only a category.

The morning alert queue had 23 items in it. Three of them were these. The other twenty were the ordinary operational noise of a 34-node network on its second full day of distributed operation. She would work through all of them, in order, at the pace the work required.

The map on the wall had three red nodes. The rest were green. The ratio would change again by morning — some red to green, some green to red, some colors she had not yet added to the legend.

She went back to the queue.

chapters.title: Chapter 2: Outside Supervision
chapters.text:
Fay's authorization request entered the coordination queue at 0810.

She sent it from a transit hub two districts west of the buffer zone, where she was already buying a platform ticket. The request was brief: *Transit authorization for field repair — buffer zone node, isolated, 340 residents, Timelost registry data, no backup. ETA midday. Parts in kit. Request expedited approval.*

The coordination queue processed requests in received order. There were fourteen items ahead of hers. The estimated processing time was displayed as a range: two to six hours depending on whether any items required committee review. Fay read this, bought her ticket, and boarded the 0823 platform transit.

The coordination queue returned its approval at 1340. By then, she was already on her way back.

---

Fay had been in and out of the coordination framework since before it had that name — since the early reconciliation work in vol-008, when the Archive's initial eastern community contacts had required someone who understood how to talk to people who had reason not to trust the Archive, which was most of the people the Archive was trying to reach. She had spent four years in that role, which was not formally defined, which paid inconsistently, which had no official authority and also no official limit on where she could go and what she could do. The role suited her because the work she was actually doing was usually two steps ahead of anyone's ability to authorize it.

She had found out about the buffer zone node the same way she found out about most things that needed doing before the coordination framework had gotten around to documenting them: she had been looking for something else and had seen it. She had been reviewing the node list for the badlands communities — checking which ones had come online through the network in the last week — when she noticed a node registered under the buffer zone settlement number that was showing as isolated, not offline. Isolated was different from offline. Offline meant the hardware was down. Isolated meant the hardware was working but had no active network path to reach the other nodes.

She had traced the isolation. The buffer zone node's upstream relay was the eastern corridor relay — Solan's relay, which had gone offline at 0658 with a hardware failure. When Solan's relay went down, every node that depended on it for its upstream connection had lost its path to the network. Most of those nodes had backup routing. The buffer zone node did not.

The buffer zone settlement had 340 people. It had been maintaining its own records — Timelost community records, family testimony, geographic documentation from a timeline that had stopped existing eleven years ago — since long before the network existed. It had joined the distributed registry eight months ago, when the reconciliation framework began establishing contact with buffer zone communities. The records had been uploaded to the distributed registry at that time. But *distributed* meant distributed to the nodes the registry had reached; the buffer zone node was one of those nodes, which meant its copy of the data was locally held, isolated along with the node, while the rest of the distributed registry was accessible.

The community was not panicking. She knew this before she arrived because she had contacted their node operator — a woman named Ressa who had been managing the settlement's archive since the community established it — and Ressa had said: *We know. We've been managing our own records for eleven years. We know they're here. We know the network can't reach them right now. Can you fix the relay?*

Fay had said she could fix the relay. She had not said the coordination queue had not yet approved the trip, because the approval was a process and the relay was a physical thing that needed a physical intervention that she could perform and that she was going to perform whether or not the queue got around to it before she boarded her transit.

---

The buffer zone was three hours from the transit hub in the direction that transit went, and then forty-five minutes on foot to the settlement's edge. The settlement had a vehicle. Ressa sent someone to meet her at the road junction. The someone was a quiet man who carried one of Fay's kit bags and did not talk much, which she appreciated because it was the right kind of trip for not talking much.

The relay was in a shed attached to the settlement's secondary water tower — positioned there because the water tower had the height for signal and the shed had the weatherproofing. The shed was dry and organized. Ressa had been maintaining it with the kind of care that came from knowing that if this broke she would have to explain to 340 people why their archive was inaccessible, which was a different kind of motivation than routine maintenance.

The fault was a cracked connector in the relay's primary feed unit. The crack had been caused by thermal cycling — the shed's insulation was inadequate for the temperature differentials in the buffer zone, and the connector had expanded and contracted enough times that it had finally failed. It was the kind of failure that a hardware inspection would have caught, if anyone with hardware expertise had visited the settlement in the last two years. No one had.

She replaced the connector. While the housing was open, she checked the rest of the feed unit's components — the thermal cycling that had cracked the connector had also stressed the adjacent insulation layer, which was beginning to separate from the backing. She noted it, noted that it had a year of functional life left under normal conditions but less if the winter was cold, and added it to the repair list she was building in the back of her kit bag.

She added a thermal buffer to the housing. The thermal buffer was not in the spec for this type of relay unit — it was an improvised solution using materials she had brought because she had worked on similar installations in similar climates before and had learned to carry the materials. It would extend the connector's functional life by three or four cold seasons. It would not solve the underlying problem, which was the shed's insulation, which was the settlement's infrastructure budget, which was the buffer zone communities' access to Archive supply networks, which was a chain of institutional constraints that she was not going to resolve before the 1624 platform.

She added the shed insulation to the repair list.

She tested the circuit. She connected the relay. She watched the node come back online.

The coordination network registered the buffer zone node as active at 1241.

The 340-person community's registry records — eleven years of self-maintained documentation in a format that was close to, but not precisely, the distributed registry's standard — were now accessible to the network.

---

Fay ate a meal with Ressa and the quiet man and two others who had been in the record room while the repair was happening. The record room was adjacent to the shed — a converted supply space with shelving units along three walls and a terminal on the fourth. The shelving held the physical backup of the digital records: printed copies, sealed in climate-appropriate cases, organized by year. Eleven years of records in a room the size of a large closet.

She had seen rooms like this before — communities that had been maintaining parallel records while the Archive either ignored them or documented them for Archive-only use. The communities had kept their own records because they had learned that the Archive's documentation did not always serve the community the Archive was documenting. The buffer zone settlement had been operating without Archive support for so long that their record-keeping had evolved its own conventions, its own cross-reference structure, its own date formats. The records were complete. The records were correct. The records were in a format that the distributed registry would need to accommodate if it wanted to actually receive what these communities had preserved.

During the meal she explained what she had found and what she had fixed and what would need to be addressed before the next cold season if the relay was going to hold. She wrote down the parts list on paper because the settlement's terminal had been offline while the relay was disconnected and she did not want to wait for it to reconnect before she gave Ressa something concrete to request through the supply queue.

Ressa read the list and made a note. "How long before the supply request is processed?"

"I don't know," Fay said. "The queue is running slow. I'd plan for four to six weeks."

"We can bridge four to six weeks." Ressa folded the list and put it in her pocket. "We've bridged longer."

On the road back to the transit hub — the same quiet man drove, and it was the same kind of trip — Fay checked the coordination terminal. The approval for her transit authorization had arrived at 1340. The note attached said: *Expedited approval granted. Proceed with field repair.* She was already twenty minutes past the junction point by then.

She filed a completion report from the transit hub while she waited for the 1624 platform back. The completion report included the repair performed, the materials used, the additional maintenance notes, the estimated timeline for next service, and a formal recommendation that the buffer zone communities be added to the Archive's regular supply access list so that hardware replacement did not require individual escalation every time a connector cracked.

She had written versions of that recommendation four times in four years. She filed it again because the record of having recommended it was as important as the recommendation being acted on. Someday someone would ask why the relay kept failing, and the answer would be in the completion reports, dated and specific, and whoever asked would have to decide whether they found that useful or uncomfortable.

---

The completion report went to Iris's queue and Iris read it that evening, after she had worked through the three incident reports and the fifteen ordinary operational items that had accumulated during the afternoon. She read the completion report carefully. The buffer zone node's records were in a format that was close to the distributed registry's standard but not identical — the settlement had developed their own system before they knew a standard would exist, and the differences were specific: field names used slightly different conventions, date formats were locally coded, certain cross-reference structures were inverted.

None of the differences were wrong. They were all correct within the system the settlement had built. The settlement had built a system that worked for eleven years without Archive authorization or external review or technical support. It worked. It just worked differently.

She opened the registry's import specification file. There was not currently an import specification that could receive records in the settlement's format without manual correction of each field. That meant someone — her, or a node operator with the right access — would have to review each record individually if they wanted to add the settlement's historical records to the distributed network's searchable layer.

The settlement had eleven years of records. She was not going to manually correct eleven years of records.

She began building an import specification that could handle both formats, translating between them automatically for any new records submitted. It was not a large technical task — the differences were systematic, not random, which meant a mapping between them was straightforward to write. The field name differences were consistent: the settlement had used descriptive names where the registry used shorthand codes, because they had built their system for human readers rather than database queries. The date format was regional. The inverted cross-reference structure had been a deliberate design choice — Ressa's note in the system documentation said *inverted because the primary record should point to the related context, not the other way around,* which was a different philosophy about what was primary and what was secondary and which one should do the pointing. Iris read that note twice. She had made a similar design choice, with different reasoning, in the registry's own structure. The systems had arrived at something adjacent from different directions.

What the import specification required was that someone understand both formats well enough to write the mapping correctly. That required her to read the settlement's documentation carefully. She read it carefully.

What it required was that someone do it.

She did it. The import specification was functional by 2140. She sent it to the node operator at the buffer zone with a note: *This translator will handle format conversion automatically for future submissions. Historical records can be imported using the attached batch process — no manual correction required. Welcome to the network.*

She got a reply at 2253: *Thank you. Also the thermal buffer was a good addition. How did you know to do that?*

Fay, she wrote back, had known.

The network had grown by 340 people the Archive had never formally counted. Their eleven years of records were now part of the distributed registry, in the import format that Iris had built in three hours, alongside the 847 names and the ceased entries and the annotation fields that were still accumulating overnight. The import specification was now available for any node operator whose community had been maintaining records in a non-standard format. Iris had already identified six other communities in the network that were in similar situations — communities that had joined the distributed registry but whose historical records predated the standard format and were sitting in their local nodes untranslated. She sent the import specification to all six with a note explaining how it worked and inviting them to contact her if they had questions.

Four of the six replied the same night. The fifth replied in the morning. The sixth, as it turned out, had been waiting for something like this for two years and had been keeping a list of records they wanted to submit as soon as there was a way to submit them. The list was long.

The network held them the same way it held everything: imperfectly, honestly, open to being read by anyone who looked.

chapters.title: Chapter 3: Self-Interested, Partial, and Still Real
chapters.text:
Orin Sect made the call from a coffee shop he owned.

The coffee shop was in the Juncture district, two blocks from the transit hub, in a space he had purchased eleven years ago when the district's property values were still recovering from the calibration period and the price was low enough to be worth it as an investment and also as a place he liked to spend time. He had been meaning to fix the coffee for a year. The problem was the roasting supplier — he had used the same supplier for nine years and they had good relations and the supplier had changed their roasting process fourteen months ago and the result was technically adequate coffee that lacked a quality he did not have precise language for, something in the finish, something that had been there before and was now absent without the absence being definitively identifiable as the supplier's fault.

He had been meaning to address this for a year. He had not addressed it today. He ordered a cup of the technically adequate coffee and waited for his connection to go through.

Tomas Vael picked up on the fourth ring. Sect recognized the pause before Vael spoke as the pause of someone who had known this call might come and had spent time deciding what to say when it did.

"Sect," Vael said.

"Archivist," Sect said. "I understand you've filed a hold on the Juncture corridor node segment."

Another pause. "The hold is within my authority to file."

"It is." Sect drank some of the coffee. The finish was what it was. "The hold is on a node segment containing calibration records from the Act III period in the Juncture corridor. Records that include your authorization signature on four documentation events."

Vael said nothing.

"I'm not calling about the hold itself," Sect said. "I'm calling because I have a ledger I've been carrying for eleven years that has some relevance to what's in that node segment. Procurement records from the same period. My accounts were the distribution layer for several of the contracts that funded the operations the calibration records document."

"Your accounts were never—"

"Three degrees removed. I know. I built that distance carefully." He set the coffee cup down. "What I'm calling to say is that I've decided I don't need that distance anymore. The ledger goes to the witness registry as a voluntary disclosure. I'm not waiting for authorization. I'm telling you before I submit it because it seemed like the honest thing to do, and because submitting it makes the three-degree separation visible to anyone who reads both documents."

The line was quiet for long enough that he could hear the ambient sound of wherever Vael was — a room with the specific quiet of an administrative office at midday, the kind of quiet that accumulates in spaces where most of the noise is paper and slow decisions.

"What are you asking for?" Vael said finally.

"I'm not asking for anything," Sect said. "I'm telling you what I'm doing. You can withdraw the hold or leave it. The ledger goes in either way."

---

Vael had not said yes and had not said no, but Sect knew what the not-saying meant. People who were going to refuse an offer you had been careful not to make as a threat said so immediately, in the specific language of people who wanted to demonstrate they were not being coerced. Vael had asked what Sect was asking for. That was not the response of someone who was not going to comply.

He ordered a second cup of the technically adequate coffee and drank half of it and left the rest. He had things to do before 1200.

---

He had been carrying the ledger for eleven years because he had understood, from the first week of the calibration period, that what he knew about his accounts' role in the funding chain was the kind of information that became leverage when it was held and became exposure when it was shared. He had held it, which meant he had leverage he never used, which meant it had been costing him something — the maintenance of the silence, the specific shape of his operations designed around the gap where certain records weren't, the slight restriction that came from knowing what he couldn't say and building around it. Eleven years of that was a kind of tax on his operation that he had never explicitly calculated.

He had calculated it, finally, in the week after Theron Gale's upload reached the network.

Theron had built 847 entries over four years. He had timed the upload. He had named himself last and accurately, in the same format as everyone else, with no special claim on how his contribution should be received. He had written his *After* field in past tense because he had already made the decision.

Sect had read the 848th entry the way he read documents of consequence: twice slowly, once quickly, then once more at the place that had the most information. The most information was in the status field: *calibrated without consent. Still here. Still named.* Theron had spent four years naming people who had been harmed by an operation that Sect's accounts had helped fund. The three-degree separation was a fact. The facts on either side of it were also facts.

The ledger had become leverage he didn't want anymore.

This was not a moral awakening. He was careful about that characterization in his own thinking — he had been in the business of accurate assessment for long enough to know when he was telling himself a story that made himself the protagonist of a redemption arc. He was not doing that. What had changed was not his view of what he had done; that view had been stable and accurate for eleven years. What had changed was the calculation about what holding the ledger was worth.

The distributed registry now held 847 names of people who had been calibrated. His accounts had been in the funding chain. He had known that and built a distance. The distance was still three degrees. The ledger was still in his possession. The registry now had everything on either side of the distance except the bridge. The bridge was his. He had been holding a bridge that led to a thing that was now in 34 places, and the bridge was the only gap in the chain, and the chain was the kind that people with access and patience could reconstruct without the bridge, given enough time.

Carrying the bridge and calling it protection was a habit he had built before the registry existed. The registry had changed what protection meant.

---

Vael's withdrawal notice arrived in the administrative queue at 1147.

He did not accompany it with a message to Sect. He did not acknowledge the call. The withdrawal was filed in the correct format under the correct administrative code and the reason field read *classification review completed — no action required.* It was technically accurate in the sense that all the words in it were words that could be used to describe what had happened, assembled in a way that revealed nothing about what had actually motivated it.

Sect submitted the ledger to the witness registry at 1203.

The submission went through the distributed registry's standard intake process. It was in witness format because the witness format was what the registry accepted and because the witness format was, in fact, the correct container for what the ledger documented — not a confession, not an account of personal harm, but a record that named who had held what and when and through what channels, such that anyone reading the calibration records and the ledger together could follow the chain from the operation to the authorization to the funding to the distribution point.

The ledger was not exculpatory. It did not say Sect had not known. It did not say he had not participated. It said what was true: what he had known, when he had known it, and what he had done with the knowledge. What he had done with it was build a distance that the ledger now made visible.

He had spent the night before the call reading the calibration records that were now accessible through the node — or rather, reading the portions of them that had not been under hold, because Vael's hold had covered only the segment where his authorization signature appeared. Reading around a gap in a document was a different kind of reading than reading a complete document. You could infer the shape of what was missing from what was on either side of it. What was on either side of Vael's authorization was specific enough that the shape was legible.

The node segment in the Juncture district came back online at 1203. The hold withdrawal processed in forty-one seconds. The calibration records that had been under hold were now accessible across the distributed registry. The ledger entry, which had been submitted at 1203, was also now in 34 nodes.

---

Sect closed the call, which had been technically over since Vael filed the withdrawal, and put his terminal in his jacket pocket. He finished the coffee. It was the technically adequate coffee that it had been when he ordered it. He had not fixed the coffee problem today, and he was not going to fix the coffee problem today, because today he had done one specific thing and the coffee problem was a different problem for a different day.

He had been in the coffee shop, at the small table in the back left corner, for forty-two minutes.

He put the coffee cup in the bin behind the counter — the coffee shop was set up for self-service, which he preferred — and said hello to the counter technician, who was a young man named Denni who had been working there for eight months and who was good at his job and who had not asked about the call because it was not his business and Sect appreciated that in staff. He said hello and went through the back door and back to what he had been doing before the call, which was reviewing the supply chain documentation for his current operation's second-quarter logistics.

He did not feel notably different than he had at 0800.

He had told Corin, once, that he kept his accounts clean because the ledger was cleaner than the alternative. That was still true. It was also true in the other direction: the ledger was cleaner out of his hands than in them, now that what it bridged was already in the record. Holding a document for eleven years as protection, then submitting it at the moment when not submitting it was the thing that looked like protection — that was the kind of calculation his operation ran on. The calculation was not complicated. It was just specific.

He had expected, in some vague way he had not examined closely, that submitting the ledger would feel like a particular kind of relief. He had been wrong about that, or at least partially wrong. The ledger was in the registry. The hold was lifted. The calibration records were accessible. The three-degree distance was now mapped, legible, part of the record that anyone could read. All of that was true. What it felt like was the absence of a specific weight he had been carrying long enough that its absence was more notable than its presence had been.

That was not nothing. It was also not absolution, and he had not expected absolution, and that expectation not being present was something he had understood about himself for a long time.

The supply chain documentation was where he had left it. He went back to it.

The coffee shop did its afternoon business in the Juncture district. The coffee was technically adequate. The ledger was in 34 nodes.

---

Corin received a notification about the hold withdrawal at 1205. He read the withdrawal notice and the ledger submission timestamp and understood what had happened without being told. He sent Sect one line: *Ledger received. Node segment online. What happened?*

Sect replied at 1312: *I had a ledger I didn't need anymore.*

Corin read this twice. He could have asked a follow-up question. He had learned, over the years he had been in contact with Sect's orbit, that asking Sect follow-up questions about why he had done something returned less information than reading what he had actually done. The ledger was in the registry. The node was online. The calibration records were accessible. What Sect had needed or not needed was between Sect and whatever calculation he ran his operation on, and asking about the calculation would not change its outcome.

He sent one reply: *Thank you.*

Sect did not reply to that. He would not have expected one. He marked the thread as received and moved to the next item in the coordination queue. The node segment was online. The calibration records from the Juncture corridor were accessible across the distributed registry. Tomas Vael's authorization signature was now legible in that record to anyone who read it.

The ledger was in the record. Everything that had been in the ledger was in the record. Everything that had been in the record before the hold was in the record again. The network had one more node segment. The network's record of who had funded what during the Act III calibration period was three degrees more complete than it had been that morning.

Three degrees. That was what Sect had chosen to close, which was partial, and imperfect, and still a real contribution to the accuracy of the record.

It was in 34 nodes. It would stay there.

chapters.title: Chapter 4: Names on a Relay
chapters.text:
Vessa received the threat intelligence at 1800 and was in the fighters' location by 2100.

Three hours was not enough time to arrange authorization, coalition response, or a defensive relay operation. It was exactly enough time to get there herself: one hour of transit, forty minutes of walking a route she had used twice before when this part of the city was not safe for direct access, twenty minutes to reach the building and get inside. She had done the calculation while she was still reading the intelligence report. She had left before the calculation finished.

The fighters were in a building that had been a transit maintenance facility and was now used for meetings that no one wanted to connect to a specific address. There were twenty-two of them — she had been told twenty-two, and when she arrived and was let in by the outer watch, she did a count: twenty-two. The intelligence had been accurate. She noted this as a good sign for what the intelligence had also said about their timeline: the strike was planned for 0300.

It was 2104.

She had not called Corin before leaving. She had not called Joren. There had not been time to arrange an official response and if she waited for an official response the relay would be gone before the response arrived. This was the same calculation Fay had made that morning with the buffer zone node — leave now, authorization arrives after. She understood why Fay had made it. She was making it herself at 1800, with a transit ticket and a different kind of knowledge about what the relay currently held.

She had been the one to recommend including the independent Congregation members in the intelligence distribution. If the relay was destroyed, she would have been the person who put the strike plan's targets in her own network's hands. She had not thought of this during the transit; she thought of it while she was walking the approach route. She noted it, set it aside, and continued walking. It was the kind of thought that was true and was not useful until after the thing that needed to happen had happened.

---

The twenty-two were not Vessa's fighters. They had been Null Congregation members up through vol-007, when the Congregation's position on direct action against Archive infrastructure had shifted — not reversed, but moderated, moved toward the kind of indirect pressure that the coalition was now calling preservation work. The twenty-two had not made the same move. They had received Joren's messages and had weighed them and had concluded that what Joren was asking them to do was exchange one kind of constraint for another: the constraint of Archive authority for the constraint of coalition authority, which was still authority and still required them to ask before acting. They had remained outside the coalition, outside the Congregation's revised position, maintaining their own operational logic.

Their operational logic was consistent and not stupid. They had identified Archive relay infrastructure as targets that, if disabled, would slow the Archive's ability to coordinate institutional action in the eastern territories. They were technically correct that Archive relays served Archive coordination. They had developed their target list over eight months of observation. The relay in question was the largest eastern corridor installation and the one through which the most Archive communications traffic moved. The logic for targeting it was sound, given the premises.

The premises did not include the distributed registry, because the distributed registry was eleven days old and had not been in their target assessment framework when they started planning.

Their operational logic had brought them here, to this building at 2104, with a plan to destroy the Archive relay that was currently transmitting the eastern corridor's distributed registry traffic.

The relay was an Archive installation. That was factually true. It was also currently transmitting records that had nothing to do with Archive operations. Vessa had checked the relay's current traffic log before she left. The traffic log showed: the 847 names Theron Gale had submitted, routed through the eastern corridor as part of the 34-node distribution. The eastern communities' eleven years of parallel records, which Fay had brought back online that afternoon. The Timelost community documentation from the buffer zone settlement. The "ceased" registry entries — the street that was gone, the bread recipe that was gone, the half-annotated voice that was gone — along with the 341 annotations that had accumulated overnight.

And, currently being read by an overnight operator in the mountain settlement who was trying to find the word for the seasonal ceremony: the three phonetic variants that were all the registry held.

She had taken screenshots of the traffic log. She had them on her terminal.

She did not lead with the terminal. She led with what the twenty-two already knew about her, which was that she had been doing Congregation work for twelve years and had not pivoted to coalition work without understanding what she was giving up and what she was getting. They knew her as someone who did not argue from authority or sentimentality. She had established that, over twelve years, through specific actions. The twenty-two knew her work the way fighters know other fighters: not personally, but accurately.

She stood in the room. Twenty-two people looked at her.

"I'm not going to tell you the relay is not an Archive installation," she said. "It is. I'm not going to tell you that Archive infrastructure isn't a legitimate target. You've spent twelve years developing the analysis. You're not wrong about what the relay serves in the Archive's system."

The room was quiet. Someone near the back shifted position but did not speak.

"I'm going to ask you to read what's currently moving through it," she said. "Before 0300."

She put her terminal on the table in the center of the room and pulled up the traffic log.

---

The relay was currently transmitting names. That was the simple way to say it. The technical way was that it was transmitting compressed archive packages through the eastern corridor's synchronization protocol, and each package was a registry entry, and each registry entry was someone's documentation in the witness format. But the simple way was accurate and the technical way was a different kind of accuracy that did not serve the specific purpose of the next several hours.

She did not stay at the table. She moved back toward the wall — not removing herself from the room, but giving them space to read without her standing over it. She had made the offer. The offer was in front of them. Whether to read it was their choice, and making it their choice was the point.

Seven of the twenty-two came to the table within five minutes. The others watched. After ten minutes, four more joined. At twenty minutes, three more stood at the edge of the table, reading over the others' shoulders.

Nobody spoke during the reading. The room was the kind of quiet that came from a specific task being done with full attention. The quiet was different from the quiet of people who had nothing to say. It was the quiet of people who were processing something that had not been in their framework before and who were doing that processing with the same seriousness they had applied to the target assessment.

She had expected some of them to read superficially — to skim enough to say they had looked and then hold their position unchanged. She had been wrong. The fighters who came to the table were reading. She could tell the difference by where their eyes stopped and by which sections they returned to.

The relay traffic log was long. Reading it seriously required more than twenty minutes. Several of the fighters at the table were on their second scroll through a specific entry — Theron Gale's section, which was the largest single document in the current traffic, 847 names plus the 848th, plus the distribution record that showed how many nodes held it, plus the confirmation that had been sent to his terminal three days ago and received no reply.

Someone who had read only the summary of what the distributed registry was would not understand what those 847 names meant as a live traffic packet. You had to read one of them — one complete entry, with its supporting documentation, its witness attestation, its harm category, its notation in the ceased or active registry — to understand that each name in the traffic log was that weight. The fighters reading the table were encountering that weight, one name at a time, through a relay they had planned to destroy in six hours.

At 2209, one of the fighters stepped back from the table.

"Who is Theron Gale?" she said.

Vessa told her. She told her factually: what Theron had built, over four years, in a terminal in rural district 14. The upload that had waited eleven days for a connection. The 848th entry. The medical registry that had arrived three days after the confirmation. The name in 34 nodes.

The fighter stood with this for a moment. Then she returned to the table.

---

At 2253, the fighter who had led the operational planning — a man Vessa knew by name from Congregation records but had not met in person before tonight — moved away from the table and stood near the door.

"Seven of us are standing down," he said. He looked at his own group rather than at Vessa. "Seven are maintaining our position on the relay but are accepting a delay to the 0300 timeline. We'll reconvene in 48 hours."

Vessa said: "None of you are persuaded."

"Not on the relay question," he said. "You know that."

"I know that." She looked at the seven who had stepped back. Then at the seven who had not. They had all read the same names. They had arrived at different places. That was true and she was not going to pretend it was otherwise.

She did not stay to argue. The relay would operate through the night. The 48-hour delay was not a solution — she had said in the message she sent that time was not a solution, because it wasn't, and she had meant this not as rhetoric but as an honest accounting of the situation she was leaving. The seven who had maintained their position were not wrong about the relay being an Archive installation. They were not persuaded that what it currently carried changed that analysis. They had made a choice that was consistent with their operational logic, and she had made a choice that was consistent with hers, and the outcome was a relay that would operate for 48 more hours while everyone involved tried to figure out what happened next.

Time was what you had when you did not have a solution. You used it to find a solution. The solution was not her job to provide tonight. Her job tonight had been to buy the night.

She sent the message from the street outside: *Relay secure for now. Reading names bought time. Time is not a solution. Find me a solution.*

The message went to Corin's coordination terminal and to Iris's queue and to the network's incident log. Iris read it at 2310 while working through the end of the day's alert queue. She marked it received. She did not have a solution to send. She noted in her own working record: *relay secure for now — Congregation split (7/7/8) on operational question — not resolved — requires Vessa's next contact and Congregation leadership input — holding.* She added it to the list of things that were actively in motion and not yet complete, which was the longest list she maintained.

Corin read the message at 2318 and sent it to three people in the coalition's coordination structure who had relationships with the independent Congregation members. Two of them replied overnight. Neither had a solution. Both had opinions about whether the 48-hour delay would hold, which was useful context and not the same as a solution. He added it to his coordination queue and did not try to fix it before morning.

She waited until she was on the transit back before she checked for replies. No replies had arrived with solutions. She had not expected one immediately. The relay was operating. The 847 names were still moving through it, eastward and westward, reaching the nodes that held them and the nodes that were still receiving them. The night was going to hold in the specific way that nights hold: by the morning being the problem, not the night itself.

---

The relay traffic log continued through the night. The overnight operator in the mountain settlement had been reading the phonetic variants for the seasonal ceremony word for forty minutes when the traffic log showed her access close and then reopen. She had stepped away from the terminal and come back.

The operator did not know what had happened at the fighters' location. She knew only what she could see in the registry entries in front of her: three variants of a word that had no surviving speaker, annotated with everything the registry knew, which was not the same as everything anyone had ever known, and was less than what had been known before the timeline dropped, and was more than nothing.

She had added one annotation that night, before she left the terminal: *I learned this word from Ilen. I do not know how to say it correctly but I know what it sounds like in the middle of the ceremony when the settlement is gathered. I am trying to describe that. I don't have the words for the description. I am trying anyway.*

The annotation was in the registry. It was in 34 nodes. The word was still ceased. The knowing was still real.

The relay transmitted through the night.

At 0300, nothing happened.

Vessa was already home.

chapters.title: Chapter 5: The Shape Beneath
chapters.text:
Corin was on line 412 of the synchronization log when his calibration system responded.

He had been reviewing the log from the 0340–0351 event — the eleven-minute window when the hostile Reckoner's seizure attempt had run against the distributed registry's synchronization layer and been blocked by a competing signal of unknown origin. He was reading the log the way he read technical documents that described events he did not fully understand: methodically, from the beginning, looking for the place where his understanding stopped and something else began. He had been doing this for three hours. He was on line 412 of 847 when the base-of-skull vibration started.

It was specific. He recognized it the same way you recognize a sound you had heard only once and not expected to hear again — less like memory and more like the recognition of something you had stopped anticipating. The cold-copper sensation from the back of the neck down to the shoulder blade. The involuntary reduction in visual field that happened not because anything changed but because his attention had gone elsewhere, into a frequency that the room could not hear.

His calibration system had a broken Echo. It had been broadcasting rather than recording since vol-001 — the result of an incomplete procedure in the Archive's original calibration chamber, before anyone including Corin had understood what the calibration chamber actually did. He had been told the broken Echo was noise: an emission without a receiver, a signal going out with nothing coming back. He had accepted this because he had not had evidence to the contrary.

Line 412 of the synchronization log was evidence to the contrary.

---

He did not move. He was alone in the record annex at 1734 — Iris had gone to the technical wing to review the import specification she had built for the buffer zone records, and the annex was empty except for him and the synchronization log and whatever was now occupying the space between line 412 and wherever his awareness currently was.

He had never been trained for what he was doing. The calibration system's documentation described contact states in terms of the subject — their duration, their intensity, their medical markers. It did not describe what the person in contact experienced, because the people who wrote the documentation had been the ones inducing contact in others, not experiencing it themselves. He had been in contact twice before by accident and had written what he observed and filed the documents in the witness registry because the witness registry was the right container for observations that were real and not verifiable by standard evidence.

This was the third time.

He was in contact for nine minutes. He could not speak. He could not ask questions. He could only observe the thing that was responding to his broken Echo, which was not a thing but a structure — a distributed harmonic pattern that occupied the synchronization layer's frequency space the same way a fingerprint occupies a surface: not by claiming it, but by being the specific shape that left it.

He noted three things.

The first: the pattern was thinning. He could perceive this the way you perceive the edge of a sound you have been listening to closely — not by the silence, but by the change in density. The pattern had been denser before the distributed registry had expanded. Each new node that joined the network introduced its own harmonic environment, and the pattern was stretching to accommodate them, becoming less concentrated at any single point. The more the network grew, the more the pattern distributed. Distribution was dilution; dilution was thinning. He did not know if thinning was progress or loss.

The second: the pattern was not her. He was certain of this. He had been in calibration contact with Sable directly — twice, in contexts he had documented and not documented — and the texture of direct contact was specific, identifiable, a quality of attention that had a signature. This was not that signature. This was the shape of how she organized things: her specific harmonic intervals, the way she fragmented a signal so that no single component carried enough information to be readable in isolation but the whole was coherent. It was the structural logic she had used, applied to the distributed registry's architecture. The architecture had inherited her method without inheriting her.

Pattern is not her, he thought. Pattern is what she left.

The third: the pattern was doing something specific. In the nine minutes of contact, he tracked its activity across the nodes and found it concentrated around a single registry entry — the entry for Deyan Halcott, also known as Theron Gale. The entry had been receiving external queries in a volume that the registry's normal traffic did not explain. The queries were not hostile in the manner of the Reckoner seizure attempt. They were systematic, regular, and structured to extract specific fields rather than read the full entry. The pattern was not blocking the queries. It was annotating them, making them part of the log, making each extraction attempt part of the record of the entry being queried. The extractive access was being preserved as evidence of itself.

That was her logic. That was what she had done in the synchronization layer during the Reckoner's attempt: not blocked, but made visible. Not prevented, but recorded.

---

He surfaced at 1743. The annex was exactly as he had left it. The log was still on line 412 on his terminal display.

He sat with the contact for a few minutes before he wrote anything. He had learned, through the years of calibration work and its aftermath, that writing immediately after contact produced documents that were technically accurate and experientially incomplete — that the first language that came was the technical language of what had happened rather than the observational language of what he had noticed, and that the distinction mattered when the observation was the thing that needed to be in the record.

He wrote the log entry carefully.

*1743. Involuntary contact state — synchronization log line 412 — nine minutes. Source: distributed registry harmonic structure, consistent with prior calibration contact frequency range. Not direct contact. No communication. Three observations:*

*1. Enabling shape present in synchronization layer — dispersed across 34 nodes, thinning as network expands. Thinning appears to correlate with node addition rate. Not certain whether this is signal decay or structural adaptation.*

*2. Shape is not direct presence. Identified through structural signature only — harmonic intervals, fragmentation method, distribution logic. It is the method, not the originator. Pattern is not her. Pattern is what she left.*

*3. Activity concentrated on Theron Gale / Deyan Halcott registry entry. External queries detected — systematic, field-specific, characteristic of extraction rather than reading. Pattern annotating queries into the entry log, not blocking them. Making extractive access part of the record.*

*Query trail routed to Iris for review. Should be in the record.*

He sent the query trail to Iris's queue at 1746. Then he closed the synchronization log and sat in the annex for a while without opening anything else.

---

He did not know what to do with "the pattern is thinning."

He knew what to do with the query trail — Iris would trace it and determine whether the extraction attempt was something the network needed to address structurally, and if so she would address it. That was a specific problem with specific possible responses. The thinning was a different kind of problem: not a vulnerability in the network's architecture but a property of the pattern itself, a function of time and expansion. The pattern was diluting because the network was growing. If growth was the thing that diluted the enabling shape, then growth was simultaneously the goal and the cost.

He thought about this for a while in the quiet of the annex.

It was possible that the thinning was fine — that the enabling shape had accomplished what it needed to accomplish during the Reckoner's seizure attempt and that its dilution as the network expanded was simply the natural course of a distributed structure spreading beyond the original nodes it had been calibrated for. It was also possible that the thinning was a signal: that the protection the pattern provided was finite, that the distributed registry had not been designed to be self-protecting, and that as the network grew it would grow past the pattern's reach.

He did not know which of these was true. He did not have the methodology to determine which was true. He had a log entry and an observation and a sense that the observation needed to be in the record even when he did not have the framework to interpret it, because the record was what made future interpretation possible. If he did not write it down, there was no way for anyone — Iris, Tem the relay analyst, someone he had not yet met who would need this information in a year — to use it.

He did not have language for this kind of problem in the coordination vocabulary he had built over the last several volumes. He had vocabulary for node failures and administrative holds and relay strikes and hostile seizures. He did not have vocabulary for a distributed resonance presence that protected the registry by becoming less concentrated as the registry grew.

He wrote the observation down in the log. It needed to be in the record because it was true, and because the record was what he had for holding things that he did not yet know what to do with. The record held them in place while he worked out what to do.

He would not always know what to do. He had learned to be specific about that — to write what he had observed and what he did not yet know, and to trust that the record of the not-knowing was itself useful. A record of honest ignorance was better data than a confident mischaracterization. He had built the network on that principle and he needed to apply it to himself.

---

Iris received the query trail at 1749 and the log entry at 1751.

She read the log entry once and then read the phrase again: *Pattern is not her. Pattern is what she left.*

She sat with that for a moment. Not because it was difficult to understand — it was precisely stated — but because she was aware of what it meant for the registry she had built. She had built the registry format that Theron Gale had used. Theron Gale had used the registry format in the synchronization layer's architecture. The synchronization layer had adopted a structural logic that had the same origin as Sable's calibration mathematics. The registry format had been built from principles that Sable had helped establish, before Iris knew who Sable was, in the period when Iris was building a schema she hoped would exist.

What you built came from somewhere. The somewhere was in the record, if you looked.

She routed the query trail to the network's security monitoring function and added a flag to Theron Gale's registry entry: *External query volume elevated — pattern consistent with commercial data extraction — monitoring active. Entry status: intact in 34 nodes.*

She sent Corin one line: *Query trail received. Looking into it. Noted what you wrote.*

He replied forty minutes later: *Thank you for the record-keeping.*

She closed the message thread and went back to the queue, which had seventeen items in it. She had things to do. The pattern was in the registry now, in the synchronization layer, in the log entry Corin had written carefully and sent because he had learned that writing what you observed was the right response to having observed it.

She would carry it. That was what the registry did.

The queue had seventeen items. She worked through them in order. The last one, which she reached at 2140, was a request from the mountain settlement's overnight operator — a different operator than the one who had been trying to find the word for the seasonal ceremony, because the settlement had two operators now, the second trained to cover the absence of Ilen. The request was for guidance on the annotation field for ceased entries: specifically, whether personal memories of the ceased thing counted as documentation or only as annotation.

The distinction mattered because the ceased category had specific rules about what went in the documentation field versus the annotation field, and the rules had been written when the ceased category was new and when Iris had been in the middle of a crisis and had written definitions that were correct and not complete.

She wrote back: *Personal memories go in the annotation field. Documentation requires external verification. But the annotation field was designed for this — for what people know that cannot be confirmed by standard evidence. Your memory of what the ceremony sounded like belongs in the annotation field. Please add it. That is what the field is for.*

The operator replied at 2231: *Thank you. I've been wanting to ask for three weeks.*

*I know,* Iris wrote back. She did know, in the way you know that there are things people are waiting to be told it's all right to say.

She sent the reply and marked the queue item complete. Seventeen items, done.

The pattern was in the registry. Corin had written it down because it was true and because it needed to be in the record and because the record was what they had for things they did not yet know what to do with.

That was sufficient. She went to bed.

chapters.title: Chapter 6: One Night
chapters.text:
The morning tally arrived at 0600.

Corin read it in the converted record annex where he had declined Essek's command offer and where he had been reading morning tallies for three days. The map on the wall had been updated since the night before: four nodes that had been red when he arrived three days ago were now green. Seven new nodes had come online since vol-028/ch-06 — the night the coalition committed in the warehouse, which was now three days ago, which seemed longer. The network was larger than it had been yesterday and more vulnerable than it had been yesterday, because every new node was a new surface area of things that could go wrong and every new node was also a new place where the registry lived.

Both things were true. Neither cancelled the other.

The morning tally was in his queue when he arrived at the annex. He had arrived at 0548, before anyone else, because the annex was a place he could think without the coordination network requiring responses from him before he had finished thinking. He had been arriving at 0548 for three days. The morning before the tally came was useful work time: he reviewed the overnight incident flags, checked the relay status in the eastern corridor, read three replies from contacts working the Congregation question. By 0600, when the tally arrived, he was prepared for it.

He read it with the same attention he gave documents that described things he needed to understand before he could make any decisions. The tally did not require decisions from him at 0600 — decisions came later, when he knew more about which items required his specific input and which Iris or the node operators had handled without him. He had learned, in three days of working from this annex, that the most useful thing he could do with the morning tally was read it fully before responding to any of it.

---

The morning tally had five sections. He read them in the order she had listed them.

The relay strikes: the Archive relay that Vessa had gone to the fighters' location to protect was still operational. The seven fighters who had maintained their position against the delay had not moved. The 48-hour window was in effect until tomorrow morning. No solution had been sent; he had three people working on one. The relay was transmitting.

The node failures: Solan's relay was partially restored. A supply transport had reached the eastern corridor overnight, carrying one of the three components needed for a full repair. The relay was at 40% capacity. The community's parallel records were accessible at reduced bandwidth. Solan had sent a brief status message at 0347: *Running. Not full. Working.*

Ilen: still unlocated in the resettlement system. The resettlement facility search was progressing; Iris had contacts in two of the four facilities that covered the mountain settlement's resettlement population, and both contacts had replied overnight. Neither facility had Ilen in their current resident list. The other two facilities had not yet replied. Ilen was on the list. The network had not found her yet.

The administrative hold: resolved. The Juncture district node was online. The calibration records from the Act III period were accessible. The ledger was in 34 nodes. Tomas Vael's authorization signature was in the record.

The query traffic on Theron's entry: Iris had traced the extractive queries overnight. The source was a commercial data analysis firm that indexed public registry entries across multiple network systems to build predictive models of disputed records — records that were likely to be used in legal proceedings, which were therefore valuable as data for litigation support services. The firm was not hostile. It was not an Archive operation. It was an ordinary commercial entity doing an ordinary commercial thing with data that the distributed registry had made publicly accessible, because public accessibility was a design choice the registry had made for specific reasons, and public accessibility did not distinguish between readers who were reading in the spirit the registry was built for and readers who were reading for profit.

She had added a query monitoring layer. The layer flagged extractive access patterns — frequency of access, field specificity, absence of annotation activity — and added a note to the registry entry's log. The layer did not block access. Blocking access was the Archive's approach, and the Archive's approach was not what the distributed registry was for. The monitoring layer made the extraction visible. That was the tool she had.

---

The "ceased" entries had 341 annotations by morning.

Iris discovered this at 0712, when she ran the ceased category's daily summary report as part of her standard morning review. The annotations field in the ceased registry format had been designed for supplementary documentation — additional sources, community contacts, supporting testimony. It had not been designed for what 341 people had done with it overnight, which was to use it as a way of saying *I was there* or *I knew this* or *I want the record to know that this thing was not alone when it was lost.*

She set aside the morning tally and read through a representative sample.

A family member of the woman in the eastern communities who had died the same hour as her timeline — the woman whose bread recipe was in the ceased registry. The family member had written: *She used to make it on cold mornings. The smell reached the hall before she had put it in the oven. I don't know the recipe. I know what cold mornings smelled like when she was there.*

She read this and held it for a moment. The ceased category said: *subject no longer exists in any form accessible to the current Resonance Layer. The record holds the entry. Nothing else can.* The family member's annotation was not inside that category. It was beside it. It was the record of someone who had known the thing when it existed, adding themselves as a witness to the loss.

A community member from a timeline that shared geography with Venn's Landing, the street that had ceased. The community member had written: *My grandmother described this street to me when I was small. She said the baker on the corner had a cat that sat in the window. She never knew the baker's name. The cat was orange. I don't know if this helps but I wanted it to be in the record.*

A second annotation on the Venn's Landing entry, from someone she recognized from the registry's contact list — a documentary researcher who had been in touch three times about access to related records: *I walked this street in another timeline. Not this one. The topology overlaps at a point I can describe approximately. In the version I knew, the baker's shop was on the north side and had a yellow door. I can submit a full geographic description if it is useful. I don't know if it is.*

An archivist from a district Iris had not been in direct contact with, who had found the entry for the voice fragment — the half-annotated audio from the grandchild's recording of a man who had been erased fourteen years ago. The archivist had written: *I worked in the Archive's audio archive during the period when this man was erased. I didn't know about the erasure at the time. I know now. I don't have the original recording. I have notes from that period that may describe his voice indirectly. I'm attaching them. I don't know if they're useful. They're here.*

The notes were attached to the annotation. They were attached to the entry that had been in the ceased registry for weeks, in 34 nodes, still being read.

She read through forty more annotations and then stopped reading individual entries and looked at the aggregate.

The annotations were not restorations. The recipe was not in any of them. The voice was not in any of them. The street was described from the outside, from other timelines, from family memory — but the street itself was not recoverable from any of these descriptions, and the annotations did not claim otherwise. What they were was the record of 341 people who had been present, in some form, at the edge of what had been lost, and who had wanted the record to know it.

---

Iris added the "Known to" field at 0814.

She had been thinking about it since 0740, when she had finished reading the sample of annotations and had understood what was wrong with the current format. The annotation field was designed for documentation — additional sources, supporting evidence, supplementary information that could be verified. What the 341 people had submitted overnight was not documentation in that sense. It was witness: the specific record of having known, at some proximity, the thing that was lost. The two things were not the same and the ceased category was conflating them.

The field definition took her twenty minutes to write because she wanted it to be exactly right and she had learned, building the registry, that the fields that seemed simple to define were the ones that caused the most downstream confusion if the definition was imprecise.

She wrote it and rewrote it twice. The version she published read: *"Known to": A space for witnesses to record their relationship with a ceased subject — person, location, object, knowledge — without implying that the ceased status has changed. The record holds that this person knew this thing, at this time, in this way. That knowing is itself a form of record.*

She sent the schema update to the coordination network with the standard notification: *New field added to ceased category. Definition attached.*

She did not send Corin a separate message. She expected he would read it and either flag a concern or not. She did not have concerns about the field; she thought the field was correct and that the definition was honest. If he had concerns, he would tell her.

He did not send a concern.

---

At 0820, the first "Known to" field entries began arriving.

They were not from people who had been waiting for the field specifically — most of the people annotating the ceased entries did not monitor schema updates in real time. They were from people who had been trying to say something in the annotation field that the annotation field was not quite built for, and who, when the "Known to" field appeared, recognized it immediately as the right container.

The baker's family member updated their annotation to add a "Known to" field entry: *Known to me as my family member. Known to the cold mornings. Known to the smell that came before the oven was hot.*

The community member with the grandmother updated theirs: *Known to my grandmother by description. Known to me through her stories, which were not the same as being there.*

The archivist whose notes were attached added a "Known to" entry: *Known to me as a voice I may have heard without knowing who it was. Known through absence and supposition. Known to the notes attached here, which are the closest evidence I have that I was ever near him.*

The documentary researcher with the geographic description updated their annotation: *Known to me from another timeline's version. Same street, different door color. Known to me from outside, from passing through.*

The field had not existed this morning. By 0900 it had forty-seven entries across the ceased registry. By 0930, seventy-three. By the end of the morning, one hundred and twelve.

Each entry named a witness relationship with a thing that could not be restored. None of the entries changed the ceased status of anything. The ceased category still said: *subject no longer exists in any form accessible to the current Resonance Layer. The record holds the entry. Nothing else can.* The "Known to" field did not contradict this. It added to it. The entry held the thing that was gone. The "Known to" field held the people who had known it.

She sent the daily morning tally to Corin's coordination terminal at 0936 with the annotation count updated to reflect the morning's activity. She flagged the "Known to" field addition in the tally's summary note.

The ceased entries had a field that named witness relationships with things that could not be restored. The field had not existed this morning. Tomorrow it would have entries that this morning did not exist.

That was what the night had produced. Not restorations. Not solutions. A field that had not been there before.

She closed the tally and moved to the next item in her queue.

The queue had twenty-two items. The "Known to" field entries would keep arriving while she worked through them. She would read them when she had time. There would always be more to read than time to read them in, and this was not a failure of the system. It was the system working: people had things to say about what had been lost, and the registry now had room for what they could say, and they were saying it.

The morning continued. The night had held.

Tomorrow was already requiring something new.
